title: What Is Financial Viability?
nceaFocus: AS92031: Describe revenue, expenses, and profit or loss of an organisation and describe factors affecting financial viability
objectives: Explain what financial viability means for a NZ organisation
Identify and describe the components of revenue, expenses, and profit or loss
Distinguish between financially viable and non-viable organisations using NZ examples

vocabulary.definition: The ability of an organisation to generate enough revenue to cover its expenses and continue operating over the long term. A financially viable organisation does not have to be hugely profitable — it just needs to earn enough to sustain itself.
All money received by an organisation from its activities. For a business this is mainly sales income. For a not-for-profit it includes grants, donations, membership fees, and fundraising.
All costs incurred by an organisation in carrying out its activities. Includes both fixed costs (rent, insurance) and variable costs (stock, casual wages).
When revenue exceeds expenses. Profit = Revenue minus Expenses.
When expenses exceed revenue. A loss means the organisation is spending more than it earns. Sustained losses threaten financial viability.
Revenue minus the cost of goods sold. Shows the margin earned before fixed overheads are deducted.
Gross profit minus all other operating expenses. The bottom line — what is left after all costs are paid.
The ability of an organisation to meet its financial obligations as they fall due. An insolvent organisation cannot pay its debts and may be forced to close.
Accumulated savings held by an organisation to cover unexpected costs or periods of reduced income.
The not-for-profit equivalent of profit. When a not-for-profit earns more than it spends, the excess is called a surplus rather than a profit.

activities.title: Can This Organisation Keep Its Doors Open?
activities.notePrompt: Write down: What is financial viability in your own words? Think of one NZ organisation that is clearly financially viable and one that might be struggling. What is the difference between them?
activities.content:
Financial viability is one of the most fundamental questions in commerce: can this organisation keep going?

An organisation is financially viable when it consistently earns enough revenue to cover its expenses, meet its obligations, and sustain its operations over time. It does not have to be wildly profitable. It just has to be able to keep the lights on, pay its people, and continue doing what it exists to do.

Think about a school canteen. If it earns $1,200 per term from pie and drink sales but spends $1,500 on ingredients, packaging, and casual wages, it loses $300 every term. Over four terms that is $1,200 gone. If this pattern continues, the canteen will run out of reserves and close. That is what financial non-viability looks like in practice.

Now think about a small café in Wellington that earns $320,000 a year and spends $295,000. It makes $25,000 net profit. It can pay its staff, cover its rent, service any debt, and keep operating. That is financial viability.

In AS92031, you are asked to assess the financial viability of a NZ organisation. This means describing the revenue, expenses, and profit or loss with actual figures, identifying the factors that are helping or hurting viability, and at higher levels, explaining how those factors work and evaluating whether proposed improvements would actually fix the problem.

This standard is externally assessed — the exam gives you a scenario with financial data. Your job is to work with those numbers accurately and systematically. The more comfortable you are with reading financial figures and connecting them to viability judgements, the more confident you will be in the exam.

activities.title: Revenue, Expenses, and the Profit or Loss Calculation
activities.notePrompt: Draw this structure in your notes: Revenue at the top, subtract Cost of Goods Sold to get Gross Profit, subtract Operating Expenses to get Net Profit. Add one example figure to each line.
activities.content:
Every financial viability assessment starts in the same place: revenue, expenses, and the profit or loss that results from the difference between them.

Revenue is all money coming in. For a small NZ café, this might be $320,000 per year from food and drink sales. For a school sports club, it might be $14,000 from membership fees, fundraising, and a council grant. Revenue is always the top line.

Expenses come in two groups. Cost of goods sold (COGS) are the direct costs of producing what the organisation sells — ingredients for a café, stock for a retailer, materials for a builder. Operating expenses are everything else — rent, wages, insurance, power, marketing, and depreciation.

The calculation works in two steps. First: Revenue minus COGS equals Gross Profit. Second: Gross Profit minus Operating Expenses equals Net Profit.

Example: Café revenue $320,000. COGS (ingredients) $98,000. Gross Profit $222,000. Operating expenses (wages $148,000, rent $42,000, other $32,000) $222,000 minus $222,000 = wait, let us use real figures. Gross Profit $222,000. Operating expenses $197,000. Net Profit $25,000.

For the external exam, you will always be given specific figures. Your job is to use them correctly — identify what is revenue, what is an expense, calculate profit or loss, and use these figures to support your assessment of viability. Do not estimate. Use the numbers provided.

activities.statement: A not-for-profit organisation that earns exactly as much as it spends each year is NOT financially viable.
activities.explanation: False. A not-for-profit operating at breakeven — where revenue equals expenses — is financially viable. It is covering all its costs and sustaining its operations. Many not-for-profit organisations are designed to operate at or near breakeven by reinvesting any surplus into programmes. Financial viability does not require making a profit. It requires generating sufficient revenue to cover expenses and continue operating. A loss is the warning sign, not breakeven.

activities.title: Internal Factors Affecting Financial Viability
activities.notePrompt: List at least four internal factors in your notes. For each one, write a sentence explaining how it could make an organisation MORE or LESS financially viable.
activities.content:
Financial viability is affected by factors from both inside and outside the organisation. Internal factors are those the organisation can influence or control.

Cost management is the most fundamental. If an organisation controls its costs effectively — negotiating good supplier terms, minimising waste, managing energy use — its expenses stay lower relative to revenue and viability improves. Poor cost control erodes viability even when revenue is strong.

Revenue diversification matters too. An organisation that earns income from multiple sources is more viable than one dependent on a single stream. If one source dries up, the others continue. A community theatre that earns from ticket sales, sponsorship, café bar income, and a council grant is less exposed than one that relies solely on ticket sales.

Pricing decisions directly affect gross profit. Pricing too low means insufficient margin to cover fixed costs. Pricing appropriately maintains the contribution needed to sustain operations.

Reserves act as a financial buffer. An organisation with $42,000 in reserves can absorb several months of losses without closing. One with no reserves is immediately vulnerable to any unexpected cost or income shortfall.

Management quality is an internal factor often overlooked. Sound financial management — accurate budgeting, regular monitoring, responsive decision-making — helps an organisation stay viable. Poor management can destroy a financially sound business through negligence alone.

activities.title: Internal or External? Sort the Viability Factors

pairs.item: Prices set below cost of production
Strong cash reserves built over previous years
A natural disaster destroys the organisation's premises

activities.explanation: Internal factors are within the organisation's control — management decisions, pricing, reserves, cost management. External factors come from the environment — economic conditions, government policy, competition, and natural events. In AS92031, you must identify and describe both types. The most important internal factors are usually cost management and revenue diversification. The most significant external factors are typically economic conditions and competition.

activities.title: External Factors Affecting Financial Viability
activities.notePrompt: Write down three external factors that could threaten the viability of a small NZ café. For each, estimate a specific dollar impact — for example: 'minimum wage increase adds $X per year to wage costs.'
activities.content:
External factors affecting financial viability are outside the organisation's direct control. They come from the economic environment, the market, government policy, and broader events.

Economic conditions have a wide impact. When inflation rises, input costs increase — food, power, fuel. When the economy slows, customers spend less. In 2025, NZ businesses continue facing cost-of-living pressures that reduce consumer discretionary spending.

Competition can reduce an organisation's customer base and revenue. A new café opening nearby might draw away 15% of customer traffic. A national chain entering a regional market can undercut prices significantly.

Government policy shapes labour costs. The NZ minimum wage rose to $23.15 per hour in April 2024. For a small business with three employees working 40 hours per week, a $1 per hour wage increase adds approximately $6,240 per year in costs — directly reducing net profit.

Natural events are a genuine NZ risk. Cyclone Gabrielle in 2023 devastated Hawke's Bay businesses — damaging premises, destroying stock, disrupting supply chains, and reducing customer access for months. Geographic exposure to earthquakes, flooding, and drought makes natural events a real financial viability factor for NZ organisations.

Interest rate changes affect any organisation carrying debt. When the RBNZ raises the Official Cash Rate, variable-rate loan repayments increase. A business with a $100,000 loan at 8% pays $8,000 in annual interest. A rise to 9.5% raises that to $9,500 — an additional $1,500 per year in costs.

activities.title: Assessing Viability — The Five-Question Framework
activities.notePrompt: Write down all five viability questions. Then apply them to Tama's sports shop (net profit $5,800 on revenue $303,000, no mention of reserves or debt). What is your viability verdict?
activities.content:
Assessing viability requires more than calculating a single profit or loss figure. You need to consider the profit, the margin, the trend, the reserves, and the threats. Here is a five-question framework to use every time.

Question 1: Is the organisation currently making a profit or a loss? If a loss, how large is it relative to revenue?

Question 2: What is the net profit margin? A margin below 3% to 5% leaves very little buffer against unexpected cost increases or revenue falls.

Question 3: Is the trend improving or deteriorating? A profitable organisation whose revenue has been falling for two years may be heading toward non-viability even if it is currently in the black.

Question 4: What is the margin of safety? How much could revenue fall before the organisation makes a loss? If revenue is $303,000 and the breakeven point is $297,200, the margin of safety is only $5,800 — very thin.

Question 5: Are there significant threats on the horizon — internal or external — that could push the organisation into deficit?

Using these five questions gives you a nuanced assessment: not just 'viable' or 'not viable' but 'viable with limited margin' or 'not viable and deteriorating rapidly.' That nuance is what distinguishes a strong AS92031 response from a simple yes or no.

questions.question: A community sports club has annual revenue of $18,400 and annual expenses of $21,600. Which statement best assesses its financial viability?
options: The club is financially viable because it has revenue of $18,400.
The club is not financially viable — it has an annual deficit of $3,200, meaning it spends more than it earns and will eventually exhaust any reserves.
The club is financially viable because sports clubs do not need to make a profit.
The club is financially viable as long as it has members who pay fees.
questions.explanation: The club has an annual deficit of $21,600 minus $18,400 equals $3,200. It is spending $3,200 more per year than it earns. This is unsustainable — it will exhaust any reserves and be unable to meet its obligations. Option A ignores the deficit. Option C is incorrect — even not-for-profits must cover their costs to be viable. Option D confuses membership with financial sustainability.

questions.question: Which of the following INTERNAL factors would MOST directly improve a café's financial viability?
options: A competitor café closes down nearby
The government reduces the GST rate
The café owner renegotiates the lease, reducing rent by $400 per month
questions.explanation: Renegotiating the lease is an internal factor — the café owner actively reduced an internal cost. This directly improves viability by reducing annual expenses by $4,800. A competitor closing and interest rate reductions are external factors — helpful but not within the café's control. A GST rate change is an external government policy change. Internal factors are the ones the organisation can act on directly.

activities.question: A café has annual revenue of $320,000 and annual expenses of $295,000. What is the net profit in dollars?
activities.hint: Net profit = Revenue minus Total Expenses.
activities.explanation: Net profit = $320,000 minus $295,000 = $25,000. This represents a 7.8% net profit margin ($25,000 divided by $320,000). While the café is profitable, this margin means a relatively modest drop in revenue or increase in costs could push it into a loss — making ongoing cost management and revenue maintenance essential for long-term viability.

activities.title: Warning Signs of Poor Financial Viability
activities.notePrompt: List the six warning signs. Star the two you think are most dangerous for a small NZ business and explain why in one sentence each.
activities.content:
In AS92031, identifying factors that threaten financial viability is a core skill. Here are the six most important warning signs.

First: declining revenue trend. Revenue falling year-on-year without a corresponding cost reduction signals a trajectory toward loss. A business earning $320,000 two years ago and $285,000 today — with costs unchanged — is heading toward deficit.

Second: rising expense ratios. When expenses grow faster than revenue, profit margins shrink. If wages rise from 40% to 50% of revenue, profitability is being eroded even if absolute revenue is stable.

Third: thin or negative net profit margin. A net margin below 3% is risky because it provides almost no buffer. KickStart Sports in our earlier example had a 1.9% net margin — a single cost increase could eliminate all profit.

Fourth: high debt relative to income. If annual loan repayments consume more than 15% to 20% of revenue, the organisation struggles to cover operating costs from what remains.

Fifth: low or no cash reserves. An organisation with no savings buffer cannot absorb a bad month, an unexpected repair, or a slow season. It is immediately vulnerable to cash flow crises.

Sixth: dependence on a single revenue source. If 80% of income comes from one customer, one grant, or one product, losing it threatens the entire organisation. This concentration risk is especially common in community organisations dependent on a single government contract.

activities.title: Worked Example — Tama's Sports Equipment Shop
activities.notePrompt: Copy this income statement into your notes. Then calculate: (1) gross profit margin as a percentage, (2) net profit margin as a percentage, (3) how much net profit would change if wages increased by $8,000.
activities.content:
Let's work through a complete viability assessment using a realistic NZ example.

Tama owns KickStart Sports, a sports equipment retail shop in Napier.

Annual Revenue:
Equipment sales: $285,000
Equipment hire: $18,000
Total Revenue: $303,000

Cost of Goods Sold (equipment purchased for resale): $162,000
Gross Profit: $141,000
Gross Profit Margin: $141,000 divided by $303,000 = 46.5%

Operating Expenses:
Wages (Tama plus one part-time staff member): $78,000
Rent: $36,000
Utilities: $8,400
Insurance: $5,200
Marketing and website: $3,600
Depreciation on fittings: $4,000
Total Operating Expenses: $135,200

Net Profit: $141,000 minus $135,200 = $5,800
Net Profit Margin: $5,800 divided by $303,000 = 1.9%

Viability verdict: KickStart Sports is currently viable — it earns a profit of $5,800. However, the 1.9% net profit margin is extremely thin. If wages increase by $8,000 due to a pay review, net profit becomes negative $2,200. If sales drop by just 5% ($15,150), net profit falls to negative $9,350. KickStart Sports is viable but precariously positioned — one significant cost increase or sales drop away from a loss.

activities.title: Viability Assessment — The Rotorua Community Theatre
activities.setup:
The Rotorua Community Theatre is a not-for-profit organisation that stages three productions per year.

Annual Revenue:
Ticket sales: $48,000
Sponsorships: $22,000
Café bar income: $11,000
Auckland Council grant: $18,000
Total Revenue: $99,000

Annual Expenses:
Directors and performers (contractors): $42,000
Venue hire: $18,000
Costumes and set materials: $14,000
Marketing and printing: $6,500
Administration and accounting: $8,200
Insurance: $3,800
Total Expenses: $92,500

Net Surplus: $6,500
Reserves: $28,000
activities.challenge:
1. Calculate the net surplus margin as a percentage of total revenue.
2. Identify TWO internal and TWO external factors that affect the theatre's financial viability.
3. State whether the theatre is financially viable and justify your answer using at least two pieces of financial evidence.
4. The Auckland Council grant of $18,000 is not renewed. Calculate the new annual position and state whether the theatre remains viable.
hints: Net surplus margin = Net surplus divided by Total revenue multiplied by 100.
For Q3 consider both the current surplus AND the reserves buffer — how many months of expenses could reserves cover?
For Q4: New revenue = $99,000 minus $18,000. Compare to expenses of $92,500.
activities.solution:
1. Net surplus margin: $6,500 divided by $99,000 multiplied by 100 = 6.6%

2. Internal factors: (1) Revenue diversification — the theatre earns from four sources, reducing dependence on any single stream. (2) Lean administration — admin costs of $8,200 represent only 8.3% of revenue, which is efficient for a performing arts organisation.

External factors: (1) Dependence on the Auckland Council grant — the $18,000 grant is 18.2% of total revenue and is subject to external government budget decisions. (2) Competition for audiences — other entertainment options in Rotorua compete for the same audience, which could reduce ticket sales if audience preferences shift.

3. Viability assessment: The Rotorua Community Theatre is currently financially viable. It generates a net surplus of $6,500 and holds reserves of $28,000. Two pieces of evidence: first, income exceeds expenses by $6,500, meaning all costs are covered with surplus remaining. Second, reserves of $28,000 represent approximately 3.6 months of operating expenses ($28,000 divided by $92,500 multiplied by 12 = 3.6 months), providing a meaningful buffer against short-term income disruptions.

4. Effect of losing the $18,000 grant: New annual revenue = $99,000 minus $18,000 = $81,000. Annual expenses unchanged at $92,500. New annual deficit = $81,000 minus $92,500 = negative $11,500. The theatre is no longer financially viable. The $6,500 surplus becomes an $11,500 deficit. At this rate of deficit, reserves of $28,000 would be exhausted in approximately 2.4 years ($28,000 divided by $11,500 = 2.43 years). The theatre must find replacement income or reduce costs to return to viability.

activities.title: Is It Actually Viable?
activities.prompt: Think of a small NZ business or community organisation you know or have read about. Using the five-question viability framework from today, write a brief viability assessment. Is it currently profitable? Does it have adequate reserves? What is the biggest threat to its viability? What one change would most improve its financial position? You do not need real financial data — estimate and reason from what you know.
activities.nceaLink: AS92031 requires you to assess financial viability using evidence. This reflection practises the reasoning process you will apply in the external exam when the scenario gives you real figures to work with.
